title: SEGMENT 12 — Methylation calling: 5mC, 6mA from polymerase kinetics

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Methylation calling: 5mC, 6mA from polymerase kinetics
We mentioned in passing, earlier, that PacBio reads carry kinetic information — the timing of each pulse — as well as identity information.
I want to spend this segment showing you what that kinetic information is good for.
The headline application is direct detection of DNA methylation, without any chemical conversion of the sample, just from the polymerase's natural behavior as it reads modified bases.
Let me set up why this matters.
DNA methylation is one of the most important kinds of epigenetic modification.
In mammalian genomes, the most common modification is 5-methylcytosine (5mC) — a methyl group added to the fifth carbon of cytosine, typically in the context of CpG dinucleotides.
Methylated CpGs in promoters generally silence gene expression; methylated CpGs in gene bodies have more complex effects.
Methylation patterns differ between cell types, between healthy and cancerous cells, between maternal and paternal alleles (genomic imprinting), and they change over the lifespan.
Reading the methylome — the pattern of methylation across the genome — is essential for understanding gene regulation, development, cancer, and many other things.

In bacteria, the dominant modification is 6-methyladenine (6mA) and 4-methylcytosine (4mC) — these mark the bacterium's own DNA so that its restriction enzymes don't chop it up, and they also play roles in DNA repair and gene regulation.
Bacterial methylome patterns differ between species and even between strains, and are useful for typing and tracking.

For decades, the standard way to read methylation was bisulfite sequencing: treat the DNA with sodium bisulfite, which chemically converts unmethylated cytosines to uracil (read as thymine by sequencers) while leaving methylated cytosines intact.
Then sequence with Illumina, and methylated Cs show up as Cs while unmethylated ones show up as Ts.
Clever, but it has problems: the bisulfite treatment is harsh and damages the DNA, only methylated cytosines are read (5mC) — not 6mA or other modifications — and the workflow is laborious.
PacBio offers a totally different approach.
The polymerase, when it encounters a methylated base on the template, takes slightly longer to incorporate the next nucleotide.
The methyl group sterically interferes with the active site geometry, slowing the polymerase by a few milliseconds.

This delay shows up directly in the timing of the fluorescent pulses, and PacBio's software can analyze that timing — what they call the interpulse duration, IPD — and infer the presence and identity of modifications.
The information is right there, in the kinetics of the polymerase, which the platform records anyway as part of every read.
No special sample prep.
No bisulfite.
No chemical conversion.
The methylation calls fall out of the same data that gives you the sequence.
Run a HiFi sample, and you get both the sequence and the methylation pattern, on every read, for every CpG and every methylated adenine in the genome.
Free of charge, more or less.
The accuracy of methylation calling depends on the number of passes per read and the depth of coverage.
For high-accuracy 5mC calling at every CpG in a human genome, you typically want around twenty-x HiFi coverage, which is well within the normal sequencing depth for an assembly.
PacBio has explicit tools — the most recent ones use deep learning models trained on known methylation patterns — for calling 5mC, 6mA, and 4mC from HiFi kinetics data.
The applications are real.

Bacterial methylomes can be completely characterized in a single PacBio run — you get the genome and the methylation map together, which has revealed that bacterial methylation patterns are far more diverse and complex than anyone realized before long-read sequencing.
Cancer methylomes can be analyzed with single-molecule resolution, meaning you can see methylation heterogeneity between individual tumor cells reflected in individual reads.

Imprinted regions of the human genome — where the methylation differs between maternal and paternal alleles — can be analyzed directly, with phasing, because HiFi reads are long enough to link methylation patterns to specific haplotypes.
The deep advantage here is that PacBio's methylation calling preserves the link between methylation and the underlying genetic variation, in the same molecule.
You can say: "This particular CpG, on this particular haplotype, in this particular cell-of-origin, is methylated.

" That kind of three-way linkage — variant, haplotype, methylation — is essentially impossible with bisulfite-based methods, which destroy the original molecule and require statistical reconstruction.
Nanopore can also detect methylation directly, by a similar principle — the current through the nanopore is slightly different when a methylated base passes through.
Both long-read platforms therefore offer this capability, and both are widely used for methylation analysis today.

The market has split: Illumina still dominates classical bulk methylation surveys (via bisulfite), while PacBio and Nanopore have taken over for single-molecule, phased, or comprehensive methylome work.

The key insight to leave the segment with: PacBio reads carry epigenetic information as a free side-channel, because the polymerase's kinetics are sensitive to base modifications and the platform records those kinetics natively.
This is one of the unexpected dividends of real-time single-molecule observation.
The same physical fact that lets you sequence — watching the polymerase work — also lets you read the chemical modifications on the template.
One sample, one run, two layers of data.
That's a profound capability, and it's one of the things that has cemented PacBio's role in modern genomics.
